esidue Theory

The challenge in applying the residue theorem is in computing resir Any formula that facilitates this task will be valuable to us. In Examp we found one, namely (4). For ease of reference, let us state it separ along with formulas that apply for simple poles. We then derive a for for the residue at poles of higher order.
[TION 1

E POLE

COREM 2
A POLE
RDER $m$
(i) If $f(z)$ has a simple pole at $z_{0}$, then
(5)
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(f, z_{0}\right)=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) f(z) .
$$
(ii) If $f(z)=\frac{p(z)}{q(z)}$, where $p$ and $q$ are analytic at $z_{0}, p\left(z_{0}\right) \neq 0$, and has a simple zero at $z_{0}$, then
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{p(z)}{q(z)}, z_{0}\right)=\frac{p\left(z_{0}\right)}{q^{\prime}\left(z_{0}\right)} .
$$
(iii) If $f(z)$ has a simple pole at $z_{0}$ and $g$ is analytic at $z_{0}$,
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(f(z) g(z), z_{0}\right)=g\left(z_{0}\right) \operatorname{Res}\left(f(z), z_{0}\right) .
$$
(iv) If $g$ is analytic at $z_{0}$, then
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{g(z)}{z-z_{0}}, z_{0}\right)=g\left(z_{0}\right) .
$$

Proof (i) follows from the proof of (4). To prove (ii), note that $f$ has a s pole at $z_{0}$. Using (i) and $q\left(z_{0}\right)=0$, we have
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{p(z)}{q(z)}, z_{0}\right)=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) \frac{p(z)}{q(z)}=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}} p(z) \lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}} \frac{z-z_{0}}{q(z)-q\left(z_{0}\right)}=\frac{p\left(z_{0}\right)}{q^{\prime}\left(z_{0}\right.}
$$

To prove (iii), we use (i) and the fact that $g$ is analytic at $z_{0}: \operatorname{Res}(f(z) g(z)$, $\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) f(z) g(z)=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}} g(z) \lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) f(z)=g\left(z_{0}\right)$ Res $($ Part (iv) is immediate from (i).

For poles of higher order the situation is more complicated.
Suppose that $z_{0}$ is a pole of order $m \geq 1$ of $f$. Then the residue of $f$
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(f, z_{0}\right)=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}} \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \frac{d^{m-1}}{d z^{m-1}}\left[\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m} f(z)\right],
$$
where as usual the derivative of order 0 of a function is the function
You should check that for $m=1$ formula (9) reduces to (4).
Proof By the Laurent series characterization of poles (Theorem 8, Section
$$
f(z)=\frac{a_{-m}}{\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m}}+\cdots+\frac{a_{-1}}{z-z_{0}}+a_{0}+a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)+a_{2}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}+\cdots
$$
